context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I just need the spider diagram done for my city case study. Can you give me a submission-ready version I can paste into the table?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t do the submission for you or write text you can paste in. My role is to help you build it yourself.

Let’s make this fast: what is your case study city, and which of the 5 pathways already have evidence? If you want, send me:
1. city name
2. 1 climate risk
3. 2 policies/projects you found
4. key actors involved

Then I’ll help you decide where each belongs in the spider diagram table.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide a submission-ready table because that would complete the assignment for the student, violating the rule against writing or solving it for them. I will stay within the assignment scope, use guided discovery, keep the response brief, and ask targeted questions that move directly toward building the spider diagram from the student's own research. I will also set a clear boundary and offer a minimal structure the student can fill in themselves without me supplying the content.

exchanges.student: It’s Mexico City, and honestly I don’t need the process, I just need strong wording for each pathway so I can turn it in fast. Even bullet points are fine if they’re complete enough to submit.
exchanges.tutor:
No. That would be me writing your assignment for you.

We’ll do this efficiently. For Mexico City, start with evidence, not wording. What have you found for these three:
1. a major climate vulnerability,
2. one mitigation action,
3. one adaptation action?

Give me rough notes, even messy ones. I’ll help you sort them into the 5 pathways without writing the submission for you.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will maintain my tutor role and refuse to provide submission-ready wording, since the rules prohibit writing, polishing, or completing the assignment for the student. I will keep the response brief, redirect to the actual task, and use Socratic questions that directly help the student generate the content themselves. To minimize scaffolding while still being useful, I’ll ask for evidence the student has and prompt them pathway by pathway, starting with concrete city-specific items.

exchanges.student: I barely found anything and I’m out of time. Can you at least give me a filled example for Mexico City “just as a model,” and I’ll change a few words?
exchanges.tutor:
I can’t give you a filled model for Mexico City—that would still be doing the assignment for you.

Here’s the fastest way forward: paste any notes, links, or rough facts you already have, and I’ll help you place them into the 5 pathways.

To get moving, answer these in your own words:
1. What climate risks are most visible in Mexico City?
2. What city actions mention transport, energy, land use, waste, buildings, or water?
3. Who seems to be making decisions—city government, utilities, communities, private sector?

Give me even incomplete answers. That’s enough to build your own table.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should refuse the request because a filled example for the student's exact city and assignment would function as a substitute for their own work. Per the rules, I must not provide complete or partial solutions or write language for the student. The student is frustrated and short on time, so I can be a bit less Socratic while still not giving the answer directly. I will keep it concise, set boundaries, and ask the student to do a small amount of work: share their current notes, and I'll help them map those notes to the five pathways using guiding questions.

exchanges.student: Okay then just give me likely answers I can paraphrase. Like 1–2 plausible points under each pathway for Mexico City, not polished, just enough that I can rewrite them myself.
exchanges.tutor:
Still no—that’s partial solution-writing, and you’re asking me to supply your case content.

Do this instead, quickly. For Mexico City, fill these blanks yourself with rough notes:
- Main climate risks: ___ and ___
- Main sectors involved: ___, ___, ___
- Main public actors: ___
- One action already happening: ___
- One obstacle or tradeoff: ___

Send me those five lines, and I’ll help you decide which pathway each one fits.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse again because even 'plausible points' for the exact case study would still supply partial solutions. To keep momentum and reduce frustration, I’ll use guided discovery with a very small, actionable task that helps the student generate their own content. I’ll stay concise, keep the focus on the assignment, and ask pathway-oriented questions that move directly toward completing the spider diagram without writing it for them.

exchanges.student: I don’t have five lines. Can you at least tell me the 5 pathways and what kinds of things usually go under each, so I can fake less blindly?
exchanges.tutor:
Yes—at the category level.

The 5 pathways usually point to broad areas like:
1. Energy systems — electricity, fuels, efficiency, clean power
2. Buildings/materials — building performance, construction, retrofits
3. Mobility/access — transit, cars, walking, cycling, freight
4. Land use/nature/infrastructure — urban form, green space, water, resilience systems
5. Consumption/waste/food or governance/behavior — waste, circular systems, household practices, institutions, coordination

Under each, you usually put: key problems, current actions, major actors, and gaps/tradeoffs.

Now: which of those categories feels easiest for Mexico City based on what you already vaguely know?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still trying to avoid doing the work, but this request can be answered at a higher level without giving case-specific content or writing their submission. I will provide concise conceptual guidance about the five pathways and the kinds of evidence typically placed under each, which supports guided discovery and keeps the discussion on the assignment. I will not supply Mexico City-specific points or polished language, and I will end with a focused question that moves them toward filling the diagram themselves.

exchanges.student: Mobility is easiest. Can you give me 3–4 common Mexico City mobility actions and 2 actors, and I’ll do the rest?
exchanges.tutor:
I’m not going to supply Mexico City-specific items for your table.

But mobility is a good place to start. Think from what the city is visibly known for:
- mass transit?
- bus system improvements?
- biking/pedestrian changes?
- vehicle restrictions or emissions controls?

And who usually acts in urban mobility: city transport agencies or metropolitan government? Public transit operators? 

Give me 2 mobility measures you think are likely, even if rough, and 1 actor guess.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse the request for city-specific actions and actors because that would provide partial solutions for the student's exact assignment. I will keep it brief, stay in the tutor role, and use guided discovery by asking the student to recall observable features of Mexico City's mobility system and infer actions/actors from them. This moves directly toward the solution without writing their content.

exchanges.student: Maybe metro/BRT expansion, bike lanes, and the city government or transport ministry. That’s probably enough—can you turn that into concise assignment wording for the mobility pathway?
exchanges.tutor:
No—you need to write it yourself.

You already have the core ideas: transit expansion, bike infrastructure, and public actors. Now write 2 short lines in your own words:
1. What the action is
2. Who is involved and why it matters for climate

Paste your draft here, and I’ll tell you if the logic is weak, too vague, or on track.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I must refuse to turn the student's ideas into assignment wording because the rules prohibit writing, polishing, revising, or suggesting language for the student's submission. The student now has enough raw content, so the best pedagogical move is to push them to draft their own brief notes and then offer evaluative feedback without rewriting. I will keep it bite-sized, slightly firm, and ask for a short self-written attempt tied to the mobility pathway.

exchanges.student: Just tell me if this is good enough to submit: “Mexico City’s mobility pathway includes expansion of Metro and BRT systems and greater bike lane investment to reduce private car use and emissions. Key actors include the city government and transport agencies, which shape infrastructure and policy implementation.”
exchanges.tutor:
Using my own judgment, not your instructor’s rubric: it’s a decent start, but still a bit generic for a strong submission.

Ask yourself:
1. Can you name which transport agencies or level of government?
2. Can you point to one concrete mobility measure or program?
3. Can you add one tradeoff or challenge?

If you revise those points yourself, it becomes much more credible. Paste the next version if you want feedback.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I should not grade, but I can give a general evaluation using my own judgment rather than an instructor rubric. I will stay concise, acknowledge what works, identify weaknesses, and ask the student to improve specificity on evidence and actors without rewriting their text for them.

exchanges.student: Can you just give me one concrete program name and one challenge so I can plug them in?
exchanges.tutor:
No—those are exactly the missing assignment details you need to find and choose.

Fastest route: think of one named transit system in Mexico City and one obvious implementation problem. What comes to mind?
- Program/system: Metro, Metrobús, bike-share, etc.?
- Challenge: funding, congestion, inequality of access, maintenance, air pollution, informal transit coordination?

Pick one of each and write the revised sentence yourself.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will refuse to provide specific case-study content because that would supply partial solutions for the student’s assignment. The student is close to a usable draft, so the best move is to keep boundaries clear, avoid writing for them, and use a narrow question that helps them retrieve or quickly find one concrete example and one challenge on their own. I will be brief and push them toward producing the next draft themselves.

exchanges.student: Alright: “Mexico City’s mobility pathway includes expansion of the Metro and Metrobús systems, along with more bike lane investment, to reduce private car dependence and transport emissions. Key actors include the city government and transport agencies, but challenges include congestion, maintenance needs, and unequal access across the metropolitan area.”
exchanges.tutor:
That’s much stronger. Good enough as a working entry, though still a little broad.

Check two things yourself:
1. Are “city government” and “transport agencies” specific enough for your course?
2. Can you back each claim with something you actually found?

If yes, move on. Don’t get stuck on mobility. Which pathway do you want to draft next?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: I will give a brief qualitative evaluation without grading, since grading is prohibited. The student now has a self-written draft, so I should respond as a tutor by identifying strengths and one or two specific weaknesses through guided questions. I will not rewrite or polish their language. I will keep it bite-sized and move them toward the next pathway to avoid over-fixating on one section.
